sources.notes: Used to confirm the valid-x vector-space view, the prefix answer by divisibility/lowbit, and the reduction from [l, r] to [0, r-l].

reference_candidates.notes: 洛谷题解候选来源；只有实际参考后才移动到 sources。

annotation.summary: For each interval [l, r], count nonzero x such that applying a -> a xor x sends the interval to itself.
annotation.constraints: The bounds are large, so the count must be derived from the binary structure of the interval rather than iterating x.
annotation.core_idea: The valid x values form an XOR-closed set. First solve a prefix interval [0, n): x is valid exactly when x is a combination of masks 2^k-1 whose length blocks tile the prefix, giving 2^{v2(n)} valid values including 0. For a general [l, r], translate the interval by removing the common high prefix: if l and r first differ at bit p, validity depends only on the lower interval [0, r-l] under the same prefix-block rule. Subtract the always-valid x=0 from the final count.
tricks: valid x values form an XOR vector space

solution_variants.name: main
solution_variants.summary: Use the highest differing bit of l and r to reduce to a prefix interval, then compute the number of valid masks from the power of two dividing the resulting length.

solution_variants.name: linear-space-proof
solution_variants.summary: View the set of no-effect XOR masks as a vector space over GF(2), generated by all-one masks that respect the interval block decomposition.
solution_variants.complexity: Proof variant; same O(log r) implementation.

tags.evidence: The editorial proof uses the fact that if x and y both keep the interval unchanged, then x xor y also does; the count is the size of that XOR vector space.
tags.description: Reasoning about sets closed under XOR as vector spaces over GF(2), often to count or characterize valid masks without explicitly building a basis.
tags.created_reason: The existing xor-basis tag suggests a basis data structure; this problem uses vector-space closure as the main proof object.

tags.evidence: The interval invariance condition is characterized entirely by binary prefixes, all-one masks, and XOR closure.
For [l, r], the first bit where l and r differ is what strips the common prefix and reduces the problem to a prefix interval.
For prefix intervals, the number of valid masks is controlled by the largest power of two dividing the interval length.
The general interval is transformed into an equivalent prefix-style interval after removing the shared high bits.
The final answer is derived by an algebraic counting argument rather than simulation.
